[Documentation - The Zig Programming Language](https://ziglang.org/documentation/0.16.0/)\n\nSynthesis:\nIn Zig 0.16, the standard library provides input and output functionality through the std.io namespace [1][2]. You access these features by importing the standard library with const std = @import(\"std\"); [2]. A common pattern for writing to standard output is using std.io.getStdOut.writer.writeAll(\"text\"), though specific method names and handle access patterns may vary based on the latest 0.16 interface updates [2]. You can generate the full local documentation for your specific installation by running zig std in your terminal [2].\n\nHighlights:\n\u003cresult id=\"1\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eZig Documentation\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://ziglang.org/documentation/0.16.0/std/\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eZig Documentation\n\nLoading...\n\n# [src]\n\n## Parameters\n\n## Errors\n\nanyerror means the error set is known only at runtime.\n\n## Search Results\n\n## No Results Found\n\nPress escape to exit search and then '?' to see more options.\n\n## Fields\n\n## Types\n\n## Namespaces\n\n## Global Variables\n\n## Values\n\n## Functions\n\n## Error Sets\n\n## Example Usage\n\n## Source Code\n\n# Keyboard Shortcuts\n\n?Show this help dialog EscClear focus; close this dialog sFocus the search field uGo to source code ↑Move up in search results ↓Move down in search results ⏎Go to active search result\n\n# Errors\u003c/content\u003e\n\u003c/result\u003e\n\n\u003cresult id=\"2\"\u003e\n\u003ctitle\u003eDocumentation - The Zig Programming Language\u003c/title\u003e\n\u003curl\u003ehttps://ziglang.org/documentation/0.16.0/\u003c/url\u003e\n\u003ccontent\u003eDocumentation - The Zig Programming Language\n...\nZig's Standard Library contains commonly used algorithms, data structures, and definitions to help you build programs or libraries. You will see many examples of Zig's Standard Library used in this documentation. To learn more about the Zig Standard Library, visit the link above.\n...\nAlternatively, the Zig Standard Library documentation is provided with each Zig distribution. <think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Considering task documentation and database choices**

I’m thinking the task documentation should exclude external dependencies at first. The workflow persistence needs a database, and it looks like PostgreSQL might be the right choice. I need to consider task decomposition, likely one per phase with a focus on the workflow database implementation. Since the user mentioned no mocks, I can think of an in-memory store for unit tests, but the durable workflow needs PostgreSQL. It's important to keep the documentation precise regarding architecture and implementation phases.
**Planning task structure and documentation**

I’m considering around 22 tasks to organize things: starting with bootstrapping contracts and moving through core schema, platform synchronization, and various ECS functionalities. Each task needs to be executed independently, validating its dependencies without overlaps. I might end up with 24 tasks, each generating about 150 lines of documentation, which is a lot! I'll need to create the directory structure using shell commands, as I can only use Shell mkdir. Each task can be generated sequentially with ApplyPatch, focusing on clear organization.
**Evaluating agent responses and process**

I need to be concise yet precise in handling the situation. It's important to get the agents' results first, but I wonder if we should wait a bit longer. We’re currently blocked on receiving results, and the subagent is advising against polling. Maybe I could end the turn, but it complicates the response. Since notifications come only after ending the turn, I can’t use them effectively. I think we should proceed based on the architecture, even though agent completion will only arrive after the turn ends.**Checking directory creation**

I'm considering whether the system sends notifications during the next tool operation while they complete. It makes sense to run a check first. So, I'll execute an `mkdir` command to create a directory and then use `ls` to list the parent directory afterward. This will help me ensure that everything is working as expected before proceeding. It’s all about making sure I'm on the right track!</details></think>

_**Agent (gpt-5.6-sol)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Evaluating capacity decision**

I'm considering whether making the capacity decision arbitrary is a positive thing. It seems that using a power of two for capacity simplifies things, especially for sequences. If I define power-of-two as being greater than or equal to two, then I might need to account for tests that could conflict with this idea. The bounded MPMC sequence algorithm can work with any capacity that fits modulo, but the power of two allows for easier masking. I think SPSC should use a capacity of one or more.</details></think>
